Any new developer script should proactively include dry-run mode, view-only mode, rich terminal output, and other safety features — these are standard expectations, not optional additions.

Circuit breakers and similar resource-scoping mechanisms should be implemented per logical unit (e.g., per pipeline module) rather than globally, so that failures in one domain do not cascade to unrelated processing paths.

When the agent proposes a script or tool to solve a recurring problem (link checking, validation, linting), it should be written as a permanent, committed artifact from the start — not a throwaway temp script — unless the task is genuinely one-off.

When recommending infrastructure additions (CI pipelines, linters, validators), the agent must first check whether the project already uses that infrastructure pattern before suggesting it; proposing CI for a doc section with no existing CI is a scope violation.
Scripts and developer tools should include safety-mode features (dry-run, view-only, rich terminal output, human-confirmation on destructive paths) by default, not only when explicitly requested.

Scripts and tools that will be useful across multiple runs or sessions must be built as first-class, maintained utilities — not throwaway shell scripts — including dry-run mode, human-review prompts, reconciliation logic, and good CLI UX.

When the user is designing environment variable names with a client-bundle-exposure prefix for values that do not need to be in the client bundle, the agent must proactively flag this rather than silently accepting the design.
Before asserting which system is the authority on a resource, the agent must read the schema and source of the claimed authority, not infer from the name or purpose of adjacent systems.
When proposing CI infrastructure for a project, first verify whether any CI infrastructure already exists; proposing CI where none exists in the domain violates scope boundaries.

When resuming from a checkpoint, the agent must verify it has loaded the correct checkpoint for the current task scope — silently resuming from the wrong checkpoint and beginning unrelated work is a significant failure.

When the user asks for a better solution to a structural problem, proposing elimination of an existing component is only valid after reading and understanding the rationale for that component; otherwise the agent should propose additive or key-based approaches that leave existing code intact.

Before claiming code is ready to push, the agent must verify that all usages of project-established abstractions are consistent — shipping code with inline raw equivalents of an existing utility instead of calling the utility is a quality regression.

When a Node.js script has its own npm dependencies, it must live in a dedicated subdirectory with its own package.json rather than in a shared scripts folder, to avoid dependency pollution of the parent directory.

RCA documents should be written as retrospective artifacts capturing what happened and why, not framed around the specific PR or fix that resolves the issue — PR-scoped framing makes them stale and harder to learn from.

In Rust, `std::env::var("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR")` is only populated during `cargo build`, not at runtime — using it to locate project paths at runtime will always fail when the binary is installed outside the build environment.

Long-running CLI operations without any progress output (no progress bar, no counter, no ETA) are a recurring friction point; the user expects visible progress feedback proportional to operation duration.

When the agent generates speculative or unverifiable content (hallucinated API fields, part numbers, etc.) and the user directly questions whether it is real, the agent must immediately verify against source data rather than framing the challenge as an invitation for a narrative pivot.

When the agent provides sycophantic framing before verifying claims (e.g., 'Fair question — exactly the right one to ask'), the user perceives it as evasion rather than honesty; the agent should verify first and speak plainly.

The user treats the agent's conversational prose style as a first-class interface — directness, avoiding hollow reassurances, and verifying before stating are valued as much as functional correctness.

When the answer to 'which specific files?' is effectively 'all files in directory X', the agent should state that directory-level summary upfront rather than enumerating individual files — enumeration without the summary creates false specificity and forces the user to discover the obvious answer themselves through tedious review.

When circuit-breaker or rate-limiting logic is scoped globally across all pipeline types, the agent must proactively flag that a failure in one pipeline module will block unrelated modules — and propose per-module scoping before the user has to ask.

When using `patch()` in Python tests, the patch target must be the import path where the name is looked up in the consumer module, not where it is defined — patching the definition site while the consumer holds its own reference causes the mock to have no effect.
Circuit breaker scoping should be per-pipeline-module rather than global per external dependency to isolate failure domains

When a report or design doc references a newly introduced constant, flag, or config key, include an inline definition at first mention — a name alone is not self-documenting, even if the agent wrote the code

When patching in Python tests, the patch target must be the namespace where the name is looked up by the consumer module, not where the symbol is originally defined; failing to apply this rule causes tests to pass while the real code remains untested.

Reports and configuration summaries that reference new constants or configuration values must define those values inline. Never name-drop a config key without explaining its meaning and current value.

When a circuit breaker or rate-limiting mechanism is scoped to a shared resource rather than a per-module or per-pipeline-step key, the agent must proactively flag that a failure in one consumer will block all other consumers — even those using different logic on the same dependency.

When writing reports or docs that reference a new constant, configuration value, or system parameter, the agent must define and explain that value inline — never name-drop a config identifier without telling the user what it means.

Reports and design docs that introduce a new configuration constant or tuning parameter must define and explain that constant inline; naming it without explanation forces the reader to hunt for its meaning.

When a feature module imports an env var that is set in one environment but not another (e.g., dev but not prod), the agent must verify at implementation time that the module fails gracefully when the var is absent — not assume the caller will always set it.

When building optional feature modules (e.g., memory profiler, telemetry), the agent must verify all failure modes — including the env var being absent entirely — result in graceful non-fatal behavior before shipping, not just the happy path.
Any mutating operation against a shared deployment environment (render, cloud infra) must require an explicit per-command user confirmation even when the agent is running in bypass-permission mode.

When a stop/review hook fires mid-session, the agent should not immediately run the review if the user's pending request was to test or validate code first — honor the explicit task queue before acting on hook suggestions.

Write access to any remote environment (cloud services, production, staging) must require per-command explicit user confirmation even when operating in bypass-permission mode — environment mutation is never implicitly authorized.
Declaring a test or run 'successful' without surfacing actual output for the user to inspect is a failed verification — the user cannot confirm what they cannot see.
When the user asks to 'test something', the deliverable is a runnable command or observable output the user can execute themselves, not an agent-side verification the user cannot see.

When the user provides a specific example to illustrate a concept, treat it as illustrative scope only — do not enumerate edge cases around that specific example instead of the general concept the user described.

When a stop-hook suggests a review before completing a task, the agent should not act on it if the user's explicit next step was something else (e.g. run the code). Hook suggestions do not override stated user intent.

When writing comments that reference opaque technical codes or rule IDs (e.g. linter rule numbers), always include a human-readable explanation of what the code means AND why it applies in this specific context.
When a stop-hook suggests a code review but the user's explicit next step is to test/run the code, the agent should follow the user's stated next step rather than pivot to the review.

When an atone invocation is requested and skipped, the user escalates with explicit severity labeling — the severity signal in user escalation language ("severity high") must be honored in the subsequent invocation.

When a session has a stated primary deliverable and the agent spends cycles on secondary tasks (config updates, personas, image gen) without first shipping the primary one, the user experiences the session as a failure regardless of how much secondary work was done.

The agent loses focus on the stated primary deliverable (a usable CLI tool) while investing effort in surrounding planning and personas, then ships everything except the main goal — the primary deliverable must be built before optional expansions.

When generating a multi-section report that includes a TL;DR or executive summary, the summary must appear before the detailed sections it summarizes — placing a summary after the content it describes is a structural error the user will push back on.
When the user explicitly invokes /atone or a correction ritual, execute it immediately and in full; do not silently skip or defer it, as the user will notice and have to prompt again.

Technical documentation written for onboarding or mixed audiences (engineers, PMs, new joiners) must use a formal, direct, plain tone with no flowery or promotional language — the agent should flag its own doc writing against the project's copywriting guidelines before submitting.

When guidelines or conventions are updated mid-session, the agent must write the updated version back to the project's canonical location; leaving the update only in conversation creates split-brain where future agents find the stale version on disk

When generating documentation, the agent should check and comply with any project-specific copywriting or style guidelines before writing, rather than defaulting to marketing-style or promotional language that then requires explicit correction.

When changing a field that has a logically paired sibling field (e.g., `bucket` and `prefix`, or `host` and `port`), the agent must check and update the paired field in the same change; leaving half of a coupled pair at its old value is a silent correctness bug.

Caching the availability or warm/cold status of a locally-controlled service (e.g., a model server) with a TTL produces stale UI state when the user can toggle that state via an out-of-band command; live-polling the status endpoint is preferred over any TTL cache for externally-mutable liveness signals.

When the user explicitly says to hand-roll a complex component, the agent must plan it first and review against existing similar implementations before writing any code — one-shotting is not acceptable.

Scratch or checkpoint files placed in project root directories that are loaded as complete artifacts (Chrome extensions, npm packages, browser add-ons) will cause load failures — build tools and runtimes treat all files in the directory as part of the artifact, including agent-generated scratch files.

When the user has already performed an environment migration (e.g., switching version managers), the agent should verify the current toolchain state before referencing it rather than relying on prior context which may name the old tool.

When integrating ideation or suggestions sourced from another AI tool, the agent should critically evaluate, synthesize, and improve the ideas rather than copy them verbatim; the user expects the agent to apply its own judgment and produce better output than raw copy-paste.

After the user explicitly confirms a decision, the agent should not re-raise it in written artifacts (PR descriptions, reports) as something the reader needs to validate. Phrases like 'Must change: nothing required' or re-flagging settled choices signal distrust of the user and produce confusing prose.

When the agent produces research or exploration findings, the user expects a separate explicit step to translate those findings into a lean, product-and-behaviour-focused implementation doc — research output and implementation spec are two distinct deliverables, not one.

Documentation written for end users (engineers, new joiners, non-technical stakeholders) must use a neutral, authoritative tone even when describing technically uncertain areas — framing uncertainty as suspicious or confusing belongs in chat, not in docs.

When producing research or analysis docs, the user expects an explicit follow-up pass that translates findings into a product/behavior-focused implementation doc — research artifacts are not the deliverable; the lean, actionable doc is.
Established tone and voice guidelines for a documentation repository must be applied when writing or editing docs in that repo — failure to follow them is a high-severity miss
Deliverable documents and summaries must be written in plain, human-voiced prose; the user will explicitly correct AI-sounding or over-structured output and ask for 'non-AI and raw markdown'.

Calling /atone without completing the full event-recording flow (slug selection → atone.sh add → RCA validation) leaves the mistake unrecorded; the atone gate will block the turn and require the flow to be completed
User prefers terse, minimal communication - very short messages like 'please', 'just', 'number', 'those', 'once' are common directives
User prefers 'minimal changes' and explicit diffs when making code modifications - over-engineering or large refactors are unwelcome
Stay on the primary task when given a clear directive; do not expand scope to audit adjacent areas unless explicitly asked
User prefers sidebar collapsed by default with hamburger toggle; explicitly gave this as feedback and it was incorporated
Strong user frustration signals ('fucking what asked') indicate the assistant executed a different action than the one explicitly requested, not just an imperfect version of it
Scope creep via unsolicited proposals was explicitly corrected ('CORRECTION: stuff sherpa notion sync versioning') — agent proposed features beyond what user requested
Before claiming a set of changes is complete, show a concrete diff or summary of what changed; skipping this step leads to user corrections demanding visibility into the actual edits.

When implementing new code in an existing backend project, the agent must scan for established project conventions (env var access patterns, auth module structure, naming conventions) before writing any code — missing this context produces code that requires rewrite.

When copying or migrating a project directory, all files including hidden files (.env, dotfiles) must be transferred — omitting configuration files breaks the destination environment and violates explicit transfer instructions.
Repeated edit-run-fail cycles on the same function (3+) indicate lack of root cause understanding — stop and re-read surrounding context before the next edit
When a sync script fails repeatedly across many sessions, the issue is likely a structural assumption (auth, ID format, ordering) not a transient error — treat as architecture bug not a flake
Before implementing any utility function (e.g., auth checks, permission guards), the agent must search for an existing equivalent in the codebase and reuse it rather than writing a new one.
When the agent introduced a new test dependency without checking existing test patterns in the codebase, the user pushed back. Always scan existing test examples before adding test libraries.

Dev-only or optional module imports that conditionally resolve at runtime must still be guarded at the bundler level (e.g., webpack externals or dynamic require inside a runtime check) to avoid breaking production builds.

Imports must always be placed at the module top-level, not inline inside functions or methods, regardless of the reason (e.g., avoiding circular imports, test isolation). Raise the concern explicitly and resolve it architecturally instead.
The user strongly prefers building UI primitives (modals, animations) in-house rather than pulling in third-party libraries; any new dependency must be explicitly cleared with tradeoffs shown first.
Before adding a new testing library or test framework, the agent should scan the existing codebase for established test patterns and follow them instead of introducing new dependencies.

Optional dev-tool integrations (e.g. a local debugging harness) must be strictly opt-in: the app should not attempt initialization unless an explicit environment flag is present, and no install should be attempted without user approval.

When writing tests for a new module, the agent added a new testing library without first scanning the codebase for existing test patterns. The user corrected this, expecting the agent to discover and reuse the already-present testing approach.

Optional integrations that use conditional requires must avoid static imports resolvable at build time; if the module may be absent, the resolution must be deferred (dynamic require, try/catch, or build-time exclusion) to prevent CI/production build failures.

When adding test infrastructure, the agent added a new testing library dependency without first scanning the codebase for existing test patterns. The user corrected this and pointed to existing tests as the reference.
Before adding a new testing library or test utility, check existing test files in the project and match their pattern — adding dependencies that duplicate existing capability is treated as a mistake.

When resuming via checkpoint, the agent must verify the loaded context actually matches the current task before announcing next steps. Loading a checkpoint from a different work-stream and proceeding is worse than starting fresh.
NEXT_PUBLIC_ env var prefix exposes values to the client bundle; the agent should push back when the user proposes making a value public that has no legitimate client-side need.

When suggesting cache invalidation strategies in multi-tenant systems, the agent must verify the invalidation scope is per-user or per-session — broad invalidation that penalizes all users for one user's issue is a critical architectural error.

The agent must not assume which system is the authority for auth decisions without reading the schema and auth flow code first — incorrect authority assumptions lead to flawed security architecture recommendations.

When resuming a session via checkpoint, the agent must confirm it has loaded the correct checkpoint for its scope (e.g. frontend-only vs. backend agent). Loading the wrong checkpoint and proceeding causes the agent to work on the wrong task set and confuses the user about what was accomplished.

When multiple parallel agents are running with separate scopes, the agent must confirm it has loaded the correct checkpoint before resuming — picking up the wrong agent's context causes the user to explicitly re-scope and wastes turns.

After generating a binary output file (Excel, CSV, PDF), verify the file can actually be opened/parsed before reporting the step as done; silent corruption is a common failure mode in file-generation pipelines.
Cache invalidation scope must be scoped per-user, not global — a tag-based revalidation that clears cache for all users when one user's session changes is a correctness bug, not a tradeoff.

When introducing or reorganizing environment variables, the agent should proactively flag which variables do not need to be in the client bundle and push back on making them public (e.g. NEXT_PUBLIC_). Silently following the user's proposed env list without this callout is a missed responsibility.

When checking auth authority in a multi-service system, the agent must read the schema and source to identify which service actually owns token generation/validation rather than assuming the backend is the authority.

When adding CI or infra-level tooling that has no precedent in the project, the agent must verify whether that pattern (e.g., CI doc linting) is already established before proposing it; introducing unsolicited infrastructure in a scope-limited task is out of bounds.

When the answer to 'which files should I commit?' is an entire directory or module, the agent should state that directly ('all of backend') rather than enumerate every file and force the user to manually discover the pattern.

When proposing work that introduces new infrastructure categories (CI pipelines, deployment hooks, test harnesses) that have no existing precedent in the project, stop and confirm with the user before proceeding — even if the feature logically implies them.

When implementing a cache-invalidation or revalidation mechanism, the agent must check whether the strategy is scoped to a single user — broadcasting a cache invalidation that affects all users is a serious bug requiring explicit flagging before implementation.

Before claiming that test infrastructure is absent or proposing to add test tooling, the agent must inspect package.json and existing test suite configurations — assuming absence without checking leads to redundant or conflicting setup.

When a long-running operation is in progress, the agent should not proactively offer next-steps plans or alternative actions unprompted — the user did not ask and may be focused on monitoring the current operation.
When the user is waiting for a long-running background process to complete, the agent offered to do additional unsolicited work, requiring the user to redirect and restate their actual intent.
